### DOC-A1: Kubernetes Cluster Autoscaling Policy (rev 2024-11)

Section 1 — Overview. The production Kubernetes cluster uses a two-tier autoscaling strategy. The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) adjusts replica counts for individual deployments based on CPU and custom metrics exported by the application pods. The Cluster Autoscaler (CA) manages the underlying node pool, adding or removing VM instances when pending pods cannot be scheduled onto existing nodes. Both components operate independently but share a single decision window of 60 seconds to avoid thrashing.

Section 2 — HPA Configuration. Each deployment declares a target CPU utilization of 65% and a custom metric threshold for request queue depth. The HPA evaluates metrics every 15 seconds and applies a stabilization window of 300 seconds for scale-down events. Minimum replicas are set to 2 for all production services and 1 for staging. Maximum replicas vary by service tier: tier-1 services cap at 50, tier-2 at 20, and tier-3 at 10.

Section 3 — Cluster Autoscaler Behavior. The CA is configured with a scan interval of 10 seconds. Scale-up is triggered when a pod remains unschedulable for more than 30 seconds. Scale-down occurs when a node's utilization drops below 50% for 10 consecutive minutes. Nodes with local storage or system-critical pods are marked as non-evictable. The maximum node count is 120 across all pools, with a budget of 40 nodes per availability zone.

---

### DOC-A2: Cost Controls for Compute (rev 2024-12)

Section 1 — Instance Mix. Spot instances comprise up to 70% of the worker node pool. On-demand instances are reserved for stateful workloads and the control plane. The Spot Fleet Diversification Strategy selects from at least four instance families (m5, m6i, c5, c6i) to minimize interruption probability. Fallback to on-demand is automatic when spot capacity in a zone drops below the minimum required.

Section 2 — Budget Enforcement. A weekly cost report is generated by Kubecost and reviewed during the Thursday infrastructure standup. Any scale event that would push monthly spend above the approved budget triggers a PagerDuty alert to the FinOps team. Budget thresholds are set at 80% (warning) and 95% (critical). The FinOps team can issue a temporary freeze via a Kubernetes admission webhook that rejects new scale-up requests until spend is reviewed.

Section 3 — Reserved Capacity. The team maintains a reserved instance portfolio covering 40% of baseline compute. Reserved instances are reviewed quarterly using AWS Cost Explorer data. Savings Plans cover an additional 20% of steady-state usage. The combination targets an overall effective discount of 35% compared to full on-demand pricing.

---

Appendix A — Change History. This document was originally authored in Q3 2023 and has undergone four major revisions. Revision 1 (2023-09) established the baseline policies. Revision 2 (2024-02) added budget thresholds after the January cost overrun incident. Revision 3 (2024-07) incorporated feedback from the annual architecture review, specifically the recommendations around multi-AZ resilience and the transition from reactive to proactive scaling. Revision 4 (2024-11) updated all numeric targets to reflect the new fiscal year budget approved by the FinOps committee. Each revision was reviewed by at least two senior engineers and approved by the infrastructure director.

Appendix B — Related Documents. This document should be read in conjunction with the following: the Capacity Planning Handbook (CPH-2024), which provides the demand forecasting methodology; the SLA Framework (SLA-v3), which defines the contractual uptime guarantees; the Cost Allocation Policy (CAP-2024-Q4), which specifies tagging requirements and chargeback rules; and the Incident Escalation Matrix (IEM-v2), which defines the response chain for budget breaches. Cross-references within this document use the notation [DOC-ID §N] to point to specific sections.

Appendix C — Glossary. APC: Automatic Prefix Caching, a vLLM feature that deduplicates KV blocks across requests sharing the same prompt prefix. TTFT: Time To First Token, measured from request receipt to the first generated token. KV block: a fixed-size chunk of key-value attention state, typically covering 16 tokens. Prefill: the initial phase of inference where all input tokens are processed in parallel. Decode: the autoregressive phase where tokens are generated one at a time. Preemption: the scheduler forcibly suspending a running sequence to free GPU memory. LRU: Least Recently Used, the eviction policy for cached KV blocks.

Appendix D — Metric Definitions. gpu_cache_usage_perc: fraction of GPU KV cache blocks currently allocated (0.0–1.0). prefix_cache_hit_rate: ratio of prefix tokens served from cache to total prefix tokens queried, measured over a sliding window. time_to_first_token_seconds: histogram of TTFT across all requests. num_preemptions_total: counter of scheduler preemption events since server start. batch_size: the number of sequences processed in a single forward pass. decode_tokens_per_second: per-request token generation rate. queue_wait_seconds: time a request spends waiting before the first forward pass begins.

Appendix E — Operational Procedures. The on-call engineer should monitor the primary dashboard (grafana.internal/d/inference-overview) at the start of each shift. Key indicators to check: p99 TTFT should be below 200ms, GPU cache usage should be below 85%, and the preemption rate should be below 1 per minute. If TTFT exceeds 500ms, the runbook (RUNBOOK-INF-001) prescribes: first check batch size distribution, then verify prefix cache hit rate, then review recent deployment changes. If preemptions exceed 5 per minute, consider temporarily reducing the max concurrent requests via the rate limiter.

Appendix F — Benchmark Methodology. Performance benchmarks are conducted quarterly using a standardized workload: 1000 requests with a shared 2048-token prefix and 100-token varied suffixes, sent at concurrency levels 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, and 32. Metrics collected include median and p99 TTFT, total throughput in tokens per second, GPU memory utilization, and prefix cache hit rate. Results are compared against the previous quarter. A regression of more than 10% on any primary metric triggers a root cause investigation. Benchmark scripts are maintained in the repository under /benchmarks/ and are executed by the CI pipeline on every release candidate.

Appendix G — Capacity Planning. Current GPU fleet consists of 12 NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs and 8 NVIDIA RTX 4090 24GB GPUs spread across 5 Kubernetes nodes. Peak demand occurs between 09:00 and 17:00 UTC on weekdays, with average concurrency reaching 48 requests. Off-peak concurrency averages 8 requests. The capacity model projects that a 30% traffic increase would require 4 additional A100 GPUs to maintain the p99 TTFT target. Procurement lead time for GPU nodes is approximately 6 weeks. The team maintains a standing purchase order for 2 additional nodes to cover emergency scaling.

Appendix H — Security Considerations. The inference endpoint is protected by API key authentication with keys stored in HashiCorp Vault. Rate limiting is enforced at the gateway level with a default of 100 requests per minute per API key. All request and response payloads are logged to an audit trail with PII fields redacted. The model weights are encrypted at rest and decrypted only during loading. Model artifacts are signed with GPG keys held by the ML platform team. Integrity checks verify signatures before loading. Network policies restrict inference pod traffic to the gateway and monitoring systems.

Section 2 — Prefill Optimization. Prefill latency dominates the inference stage for prompts longer than 512 tokens. The team has deployed two mitigations: chunked prefill, which splits long prompts into 256-token chunks processed across scheduling rounds, and automatic prefix caching (APC), which reuses KV blocks from prior requests that share the same prefix. APC is effective when the working set fits in GPU memory; beyond that, blocks are evicted and must be recomputed. ### DOC-C1: GPU Memory Management (rev 2025-02)

Section 1 — Memory Layout. Each inference worker allocates GPU memory in three regions: model weights (fixed at load time), KV cache blocks (dynamically allocated), and activation scratch space (transient per forward pass). For the current Qwen2-0.5B model, weights consume approximately 1.1 GB in bfloat16. The remaining GPU memory, after reserving 10% for fragmentation headroom, is divided into 16-token KV blocks.

Section 2 — Block Allocation. The block manager maintains a free list and allocates blocks on demand as sequences grow. When the free list is exhausted, the scheduler preempts the lowest-priority running sequence, swapping its blocks to CPU memory. Preemption events are logged and trigger a counter metric (vllm_preemptions). Under sustained pressure, the system may enter a thrashing state where sequences are repeatedly preempted and resumed.

---

### DOC-C2: KV Cache Strategy and LMCache (rev 2025-02)

Section 1 — Prefix Caching. vLLM's APC retains computed KV blocks in a hash-indexed cache. On each new request the engine checks whether any prefix of the prompt matches cached blocks and skips recomputation for matched segments. Eviction follows a least-recently-used (LRU) policy scoped to the GPU block pool. Under high concurrency the cache working set exceeds the pool size and the effective hit rate degrades from over 90% to below 50%.

Section 2 — LMCache Integration. LMCache extends the caching hierarchy by persisting evicted KV blocks to CPU memory (and optionally to SSD or a remote store). When a cache miss occurs at the GPU level, the engine checks CPU memory before falling back to recomputation. The PCIe transfer cost for retrieving a 16-token block from CPU is approximately 0.15ms, compared to 2-5ms for recomputing the same block. The trade-off is additional CPU memory consumption and a small latency floor that pure GPU caching avoids at low concurrency.

Section 3 — Tiered Eviction. The three-tier hierarchy (GPU → CPU → recompute) is managed by a unified eviction controller. Hot blocks remain on GPU, warm blocks migrate to CPU pinned memory, and cold blocks are discarded entirely. The migration threshold is configurable via the LMCACHE_TIER_THRESHOLD environment variable.

### DOC-E1: Service Mesh Configuration (rev 2025-01)

Section 1 — Architecture. The production service mesh uses Istio 1.20 with sidecar injection enabled for all namespaces except kube-system and monitoring. Envoy proxies handle mTLS, traffic routing, and observability. The control plane runs three istiod replicas across availability zones for high availability.

Section 2 — Traffic Policies. Retry policies are configured per-service with a maximum of 3 attempts and exponential backoff starting at 25ms. Circuit breakers trip after 5 consecutive 5xx errors within a 10-second window. Outlier detection ejects hosts with more than 3 errors in 30 seconds and restores them after 60 seconds. Connection pools are limited to 1024 TCP connections per upstream host.

### DOC-A1: Kubernetes Cluster Autoscaling Policy (rev 2024-11)

Section 1 — Overview. The production Kubernetes cluster uses a two-tier autoscaling strategy. The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) adjusts replica counts for individual deployments based on CPU and custom metrics exported by the application pods. The Cluster Autoscaler (CA) manages the underlying node pool, adding or removing VM instances when pending pods cannot be scheduled onto existing nodes. Both components operate independently but share a single decision window of 60 seconds to avoid thrashing.

Section 2 — HPA Configuration. Each deployment declares a target CPU utilization of 65% and a custom metric threshold for request queue depth. The HPA evaluates metrics every 15 seconds and applies a stabilization window of 300 seconds for scale-down events. Minimum replicas are set to 2 for all production services and 1 for staging. Maximum replicas vary by service tier: tier-1 services cap at 50, tier-2 at 20, and tier-3 at 10.

Section 3 — Cluster Autoscaler Behavior. The CA is configured with a scan interval of 10 seconds. Scale-up is triggered when a pod remains unschedulable for more than 30 seconds. Scale-down occurs when a node's utilization drops below 50% for 10 consecutive minutes. Nodes with local storage or system-critical pods are marked as non-evictable. The maximum node count is 120 across all pools, with a budget of 40 nodes per availability zone.

---

### DOC-A2: Cost Controls for Compute (rev 2024-12)

Section 1 — Instance Mix. Spot instances comprise up to 70% of the worker node pool. On-demand instances are reserved for stateful workloads and the control plane. The Spot Fleet Diversification Strategy selects from at least four instance families (m5, m6i, c5, c6i) to minimize interruption probability. Fallback to on-demand is automatic when spot capacity in a zone drops below the minimum required.

Section 2 — Budget Enforcement. A weekly cost report is generated by Kubecost and reviewed during the Thursday infrastructure standup. Any scale event that would push monthly spend above the approved budget triggers a PagerDuty alert to the FinOps team. Budget thresholds are set at 80% (warning) and 95% (critical). The FinOps team can issue a temporary freeze via a Kubernetes admission webhook that rejects new scale-up requests until spend is reviewed.

Section 3 — Reserved Capacity. The team maintains a reserved instance portfolio covering 40% of baseline compute. Reserved instances are reviewed quarterly using AWS Cost Explorer data. Savings Plans cover an additional 20% of steady-state usage. The combination targets an overall effective discount of 35% compared to full on-demand pricing.

---

### DOC-C1: GPU Memory Management (rev 2025-02)

Section 1 — Memory Layout. Each inference worker allocates GPU memory in three regions: model weights (fixed at load time), KV cache blocks (dynamically allocated), and activation scratch space (transient per forward pass). For the current Qwen2-0.5B model, weights consume approximately 1.1 GB in bfloat16. The remaining GPU memory, after reserving 10% for fragmentation headroom, is divided into 16-token KV blocks.

Section 2 — Block Allocation. The block manager maintains a free list and allocates blocks on demand as sequences grow. When the free list is exhausted, the scheduler preempts the lowest-priority running sequence, swapping its blocks to CPU memory. Preemption events are logged and trigger a counter metric (vllm_preemptions). Under sustained pressure, the system may enter a thrashing state where sequences are repeatedly preempted and resumed.

On each new request the engine checks whether any prefix of the prompt matches cached blocks and skips recomputation for matched segments. Under high concurrency the cache working set exceeds the pool size and the effective hit rate degrades from over 90% to below 50%.

Section 2 — LMCache Integration. LMCache extends the caching hierarchy by persisting evicted KV blocks to CPU memory (and optionally to SSD or a remote store). Section 3 — Tiered Eviction. The three-tier hierarchy (GPU → CPU → recompute) is managed by a unified eviction controller. Hot blocks remain on GPU, warm blocks migrate to CPU pinned memory, and cold blocks are discarded entirely. Both components operate independently but share a single decision window of 60 seconds to avoid thrashing.

Section 3 — Cluster Autoscaler Behavior. The CA is configured with a scan interval of 10 seconds. Scale-up is triggered when a pod remains unschedulable for more than 30 seconds. Scale-down occurs when a node's utilization drops below 50% for 10 consecutive minutes. Nodes with local storage or system-critical pods are marked as non-evictable. ### DOC-B1: Inference Pipeline Latency Budget (rev 2025-01)

Section 1 — End-to-End Budget. The total latency target for a single inference request from API gateway to client response is 800ms at p99. This budget is split across four stages: network ingress and TLS termination (15ms), request routing and load balancing (10ms), model inference including prefill and decode (700ms), and response serialization and egress (75ms). Each stage owner is responsible for staying within their allocation.

Section 2 — Prefill Optimization. Prefill latency dominates the inference stage for prompts longer than 512 tokens. The team has deployed two mitigations: chunked prefill, which splits long prompts into 256-token chunks processed across scheduling rounds, and automatic prefix caching (APC), which reuses KV blocks from prior requests that share the same prefix. APC is effective when the working set fits in GPU memory; beyond that, blocks are evicted and must be recomputed. Current measurements show APC hit rates of 92% under normal traffic and 47% during peak bursts.

---

### DOC-B2: Decode Throughput and Monitoring (rev 2025-01)

Section 1 — Decode Throughput. Decode is memory-bandwidth bound. Each generated token requires a full pass over the KV cache. The team targets a decode throughput of at least 40 tokens per second per request. Both components operate independently but share a single decision window of 60 seconds to avoid thrashing.

Section 3 — Cluster Autoscaler Behavior. The CA is configured with a scan interval of 10 seconds. Scale-up is triggered when a pod remains unschedulable for more than 30 seconds. Scale-down occurs when a node's utilization drops below 50% for 10 consecutive minutes. Nodes with local storage or system-critical pods are marked as non-evictable. The team has deployed two mitigations: chunked prefill, which splits long prompts into 256-token chunks processed across scheduling rounds, and automatic prefix caching (APC), which reuses KV blocks from prior requests that share the same prefix. APC is effective when the working set fits in GPU memory; beyond that, blocks are evicted and must be recomputed. The scheduler enforces a soft cap of 24 concurrent decodes with overflow queuing.
